data.title: Intel's $25B Terafab Deal - Your Chance To Cash Out
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Intel Corporation (INTC) recently announced it is joining Elon Musk’s $25B grand vision of making leading-edge chips on American soil.

This announcement has strong long-term impacts for Intel to get its foot back in the game of competing as a leading-edge foundry it once was. At the same time, the Terafab deal gives Intel a potential revenue anchor by bringing in external demand for its 18A process node.

But the earliest production roadmaps for kickstarting chip manufacturing at Terafab are only in 2027, and those roadmaps aren’t even official yet. Meanwhile, Intel is still stuck with an outlook that indicates low single-digit growth.

While I am happy about my massive call on Intel last year, I am downgrading my view on Intel to a neutral stance, advising investors to start fading any rally from here on.

What Tesla’s Terafab Venture Does for Intel

With the arrival of LBT (Lip-Bu Tan, Intel’s current CEO) last year, I was extremely encouraged by LBT’s strategic initiatives to first arrest the rapid margin declines and shore up its balance sheet. At the time, I believed markets were still mispricing Intel’s new roadmap to conserve costs and protect shareholders, which I noted in July last year. Since my upgraded coverages last year, Intel’s shares are up 160%.

There was speculation last year about management cancelling its 18A node due to low yields, something I had previously expressed mixed feelings about. But over the last many months, Intel has made meaningful improvements to the yield on its 18A process node and gotten their production yield to a point where the new Core Ultra Series 3 chips, internally known as "Panther Lake," have already started shipping and will hit mass production in H2 2026. This coincides with the 7-8% per month yield improvement LBT revealed in the Q4 earnings call a few months ago.

Progress on the 18A node was improving management’s confidence about reliability to such an extent that management believed 18A was ready for external customers, as revealed by management at an investor conference last month.

In that conference, management reiterated a recent strategic foundry roadmap—keep Intel’s more advanced 14A node for internal production and the more reliable 18A for external customers while leaning in deeper on ancillary sources of revenue such as their EMIB (Embedded Multi-die Interconnect Bridge) chip packaging. A separate report by Digitimes also revealed that Nvidia (NVDA) would utilize its 5% stake in Intel as leverage to secure Intel’s 18A/14. A manufacturing capacity for its Feynman GPU to be launched in 2028 as well as use Intel’s EMIB.

Finally, we have a pivotal announcement made by Intel yesterday where the company has been signed up as a foundry partner to Tesla’s (TSLA) Terafab vision to help “design, fabricate, and package" 1 terawatt of chips annually. This is great for Intel’s long-term plans to take back control of the flailing narrative that Intel lost its lead as a dominant global chip foundry.

The opportunity to generate revenue by lending its IP to this grand vision of producing chips at the scale of 1 terawatt per year and participating at all crucial stages of the chip-making process is incredible for Intel’s future.

Too Much of the Future Baked Into Intel’s Stock Today

But I believe a majority of the future revenue that markets are excited about is already baked into the stock today.

Take Terafab, for instance. This report by Electrek notes that volume production at Terafab is only slated for CY27, which does not necessarily indicate that revenues from Terafab will be seen in Intel’s topline in CY26. Also, Electrek notes that there is a lot of potential for delay and orchestration, which could impact Terafab’s production timelines, affecting Intel’s ability to recognize revenues from its partnership with the Terafab vision. Electrek cited Tesla’s Battery Day setbacks as an example of how Terafab could see delays.

Moreover, it's extremely challenging to design and manufacture a semiconductor chip, let alone leading-edge chips, at the same time. Few companies have gotten it right in the past, and Intel should know better. Without taking away the credit to Tesla's Elon Musk for addressing the supply problem, the odds of getting Terafab up and running within a year are gargantuan, in my opinion. As this analyst correctly states, there are critical stages such as process integration, lithography, etching, deposition, contamination control, equipment engineering, and the all-important yield management to get this right, and Musk is attempting to do this over the next year at Terafab. Therefore, I believe Intel's stock is running way ahead of itself on just this news.

Intel’s EMIB packaging is also a source of optimism since management revealed in January's Q4 call that their customer engagements on advanced packaging could fetch Intel “well north of $1 billion.” Management’s projections on EMIB revenue echoed this analyst’s previous projection that Intel’s EMIB revenues could touch ~$1 billion in revenues, but that is only expected next year.

At the time of writing, all these announcements, strategic partnerships, and customer engagements have done little to move the needle on the revenue outlook this year, which remains mostly flat. Markets are projecting ~$53-54B in CY26 revenues for Intel.

For now, I believe that Intel has achieved part 1 of its goals to arrest margins and restore confidence to investors of protecting shareholder value.

Given this crucial step that Intel has taken already, I believe markets will switch valuation strategies to now value the company as if its business were as usual, with their sights on Intel’s forward revenues. This is the next part of Intel's goals ahead, in my view.

So far, those revenue estimates have not moved at all, leaving Intel with extremely bloated sales multiples (EV/forward sales) of ~5x FY26 and FY27 sales. Currently, markets are estimating Intel’s CY26 revenues of ~$54B, up 1.6%, while estimating ~$57B in revenues, up 7.5%.

For example, Intel’s global rival, Taiwan Semiconductor, or TSMC (TSM), trades at ~11x CY26 sales expected to grow ~30%. If I directly compare Intel’s forward outlook to TSMC's, I believe Intel should be valued at closer to ~4x forward sales, implying a potential downside of at least 14-15% ahead. My estimation is purely based on the notion that while Intel’s current transformation is clearing the path to a bright future, too much of the future is being priced into today’s value of Intel.

Just like I noticed the mispricing in Intel last year, leading me to upgrade the shares, I am noticing a similar overpricing of the future in Intel, allowing investors to trim some of their exposure to Intel.

Takeaway

While Intel is leading the charge and moving ahead on its goals to deliver a sustained inflection, the resulting exuberance from the investor community is leading to Intel’s shares trading at rich valuation premiums. Intel’s Terafab announcement with Tesla has further exacerbated the richness in those valuation premiums, giving prudent investors a chance to start trimming their exposure as Intel’s shares keep rallying higher.

I am turning neutral with my outlook on INTC shares.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sabine Royalty Trust Appears Slightly Overvalued
data.description:
About five and a half years ago, I recommended buying Sabine Royalty Trust (SBR) for its exceptional business performance and my expectations for a strong recovery from the pandemic. The stock has vastly outperformed the S&P 500 since that article, as it has offered a total return of 329%, whereas the S&P 500 has doubled. I reiterated my bullish thesis about six months later for similar reasons. Since then, the stock has offered a total return of 285%, whereas the S&P 500 has gained 64%.

Due to the steep rally of Sabine Royalty Trust after these two articles, I lowered my rating from “buy” to “hold” in the fall of 2021. However, the change of the rating proved premature, as the oil and gas trust has offered a total return of 190% since that article (vs. +47% of the S&P 500). About three and a half years ago, I recommended selling Sabine Royalty Trust, primarily due to my expectations that the price of oil would incur a sharp decrease off the highs it recorded shortly after the invasion of Russia in Ukraine. Indeed, the stock has pronouncedly underperformed the broad market since that article, as it has offered a total return of only 31%, whereas the S&P 500 has advanced 75%.

Since then, I have reiterated my sell rating five times with similar success, with the exception of the last two times. Since I reiterated my sell rating in mid-2024 and in September 2024, Sabine Royalty Trust has outperformed the S&P 500 by 11% and 24%, respectively. The outperformance has resulted not only from the unforeseeable war in Iran but also from the strong business performance of the oil and gas trust (more on this later). The big question is whether Sabine Royalty Trust is attractive or investors should look elsewhere for great returns.

Business overview

Sabine Royalty Trust is a high-quality oil and gas trust, which was founded in 1983 and has royalty interests in properties in Florida, Louisiana, Mississippi, New Mexico, Oklahoma, and Texas. At inception, the trust had an expected lifetime of 9-10 years, but it has surpassed that estimate by an impressive margin, as it remains at full strength 43 years after its foundation. This performance record is certainly impressive, particularly given the static status of the assets of the trust, i.e., the trust does not have the right to acquire new properties. The key factor behind the longevity of Sabine Royalty Trust is the great technological progress that has taken place in the recovery of oil.

Due to the natural decline of oilfields, the reserves of an oil and gas trust are expected to decline over the long run. However, Sabine Royalty Trust has exhibited remarkable resilience in this aspect. More precisely, the proven reserves of the trust have grown 40% over the last three years, from 13.856 to 19.433 million barrels of oil equivalent. This is undoubtedly an impressive achievement. The amount of reserves is greatly affected by the prevailing prices of oil and gas, as high prices render some reserves economical to extract, whereas low prices render the same reserves uneconomical. However, the above performance has not resulted from a change in prices, as the price of oil was approximately 20% lower at the end of 2025 than it was at the end of 2022.

In late February, Sabine Royalty Trust reported its results for the full year 2025. Its oil and gas volumes decreased 4% and 17%, respectively, over the prior year. The average realized price of natural gas increased 39%, but the average realized price of oil declined 16%. As a result, royalty income decreased 7%, and total annual distributions dipped 5.5%, from $5.45 to $5.15.

The lackluster performance in 2025 partly resulted from weak oil prices, which were caused by a global supply glut, as OPEC ramped up its production aggressively after years of tight production quotas. However, the situation has completely changed this year. The ongoing war in Iran has caused the price of oil to skyrocket due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Under normal conditions, nearly 20% of global oil output used to pass through these straits. Consequently, the closure of the Strait of Hormuz by Iran has caused an unprecedented supply shock.

The sky-high oil prices have not been reflected in the monthly distributions of Sabine Royalty Trust yet, as it takes 2-3 months for the prevailing prices to be reflected in the distributions of the trust. Therefore, Sabine Royalty Trust is likely to greatly increase its distributions in the upcoming months. Based on its latest monthly distribution of $0.3250, the stock is offering an annualized distribution yield of 5.1%, but its yield is likely to increase in the near future thanks to the tailwind of a nearly 3-year high price of oil.

On the other hand, the odds favor a reopening of the Strait of Hormuz sooner or later, as all the parties involved in the ongoing war are bleeding severely, and hence the current situation is unsustainable. Iran is being bombarded harshly while the U.S. government is being hurt by sky-high gasoline prices, which have rendered consumers upset. Even worse, if the price of oil remains excessive for months, it is likely to cause a resurgence of inflation around the globe, including the U.S. Given the upcoming midterm elections, the U.S. government will do its best to prevent a resurgence of inflation. Indeed, since the onset of the war in late February, the U.S. government has shifted its tone and has been trying to put an end to the war.

Moreover, all the oil producers in the Middle East have been forced to drastically reduce their output, as they cannot export it with the straits closed. These countries rely heavily on oil revenues to fund their budgets, and hence they have been suffering from the closure of the straits. To cut a long story short, all the parties involved in the war are bleeding harshly, and hence they are likely to do their best to resolve the crisis. President Trump just announced that he will suspend the attack on Iran for two weeks. While nothing is final yet, this is certainly a major step towards the reopening of Hormuz. The price of oil plunged 16% on the news.

Overall, it is hard to predict when the war in Iran will end, but it is likely that all the countries involved will do their best to terminate the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz. When that happens, the price of oil is likely to begin to revert towards its pre-war levels. Therefore, the tailwind from the war in Iran is likely to prove short-lived for Sabine Royalty Trust.

Valuation

As an oil and gas trust, Sabine Royalty Trust distributes its entire net income to its unitholders, and hence it has a payout ratio of 100% (D=E). Therefore, there is no need to examine price-to-earnings ratios (P/E). Instead, the evaluation of the stock can be simply based on the distribution yield (D/P) of the stock. More precisely, one can evaluate the stock by comparing its current distribution yield to its historical average yield.

As mentioned above, Sabine Royalty Trust is currently offering an annualized distribution yield of 5.1%. This yield is lower than the 10-year average distribution yield of 8.2% of the stock.

This means that the stock price will have to decrease for the distribution yield to become aligned with the historical yield. Therefore, the stock appears somewhat richly valued right now.

As mentioned above, the yield of Sabine Royalty Trust is likely to increase significantly in the upcoming months, as the high price of oil will begin to be reflected in the distributions of the trust. However, this rally is unlikely to last beyond this year, given the efforts of the U.S., Iran, and the other countries in the Middle East to put an end to the war.

On the other hand, as mentioned above, Sabine Royalty Trust is a high-quality trust, with an exceptional performance in the growth of reserves. Buying this stock at a price that corresponds to a 5.1% yield is not an extremely risky investing decision. Nevertheless, the stock appears somewhat richly valued, as it is offering a much lower yield than its historical average yield. Given also the high cyclicality of this trust, which results from the cycles of the price of oil, I recommend waiting for a correction of the stock towards the technical support of $65 before considering a purchase.

Final thoughts

The oil industry is characterized by dramatic boom-and-bust cycles. For instance, the price of oil plunged to deep negative levels in 2020 due to the pandemic but surged to a 14-year high in early 2022 due to the onset of the war in Ukraine. Given these dramatic swings, I prefer investing in oil stocks only during downturns. In this way, the investment risk is greatly reduced while the expected return is excessive whenever the recovery of the sector shows up. The 329% total return of Sabine Royalty Trust since my first article, in 2020, reflects the merits of this strategy. While Sabine Royalty Trust does not appear grossly overvalued right now, I recommend waiting for a significantly lower entry point.

data.title: Philip Morris: The Market Is Underpricing The Smoke-Free Transformation
data.description:
Philip Morris International (PM) is executing what I believe is the most important business model transformation in the consumer packaged goods sector today. When I look across the consumer staples segment I see a sector largely characterized by low single-digit organic growth, margin pressure from input costs, and stagnant volume trends.PM is challenging the narrative as its simultaneously growing its top-line at a high single digit organic growth rate while expanding margins through the shift toward smoke-free products which is leading to forward earnings growth in the double digits. That combination is exceptionally rare in this market environment. I believe the bull case for PM is still intact as they have built a multi-year growth narrative around IQOS heated tobacco and ZYN nicotine pouches. As smoke-free volumes grow and margins expand I believe that we will see PM's cash flow increase which could lead to future dividend increases and a repurchase plan being implemented. This isn't a turnaround play and the tobacco narrative should be self correcting as PM has delivered 5 consecutive years of YoY revenue growth since 2020 which is proving their strategic plan and their execution is working. I am bullish on PM and believe that the pullback is an opportunity to lock in a dividend yield that is now over 3.5%.

My previous article about Philip Morris

At the end of September I had written an article on PM (can be read here). I had discussed how PM delivered a strong Q2 with record revenues, robust smoke-free product growth, and expanding operating margins. I felt that despite their operational momentum PM's valuation appeared stretched versus peers Altria Group (MO) and British American Tobacco (BTI) which in my mind made the risk/reward less attractive. After going through their Q4 results and the 2026 guidance I am upgrading my investment thesis back to bullish as I believe that PM's execution on its product and revenue mix beyond combustible tobacco products is working. PM is a leader in the space and while the regulatory and legal risks are significant I still think PM can continue to grow and deliver for shareholders.

Philip Morris delivered an incredible 2025

PM's numbers across every major financial and operational metric reinforce my thesis that its succeeding on its product transformation.PM delivered $40.6 billion in total revenue in 2025 which is up 7.3% YoY and marks the 5th consecutive year of YoY revenue growth. The revenue growth in 2025 was driven by a $1.5 billion favorable pricing variance predominantly from combustible tobacco and $920 million in favorable volume mix driven by higher smoke-free product volumes. PM's gross profit expanded 11.1% to $27.3 billion which is notable because that growth rate materially exceeds the revenue growth rate which reflects the higher margin smokeless products become a larger portion of the revenue mix. PM's bottom lines also grew a dramatic amount as their 2025 EBITDA came in at $18.73 billion which was an increase of 24.8% ($3.72 billion) YoY. PM's net income jumped by 60.80% ($4.29 billion) YoY to $11.35 billion which led to the 2nd consecutive year of double-digit EPS growth.

On an operational front PM was able to increase its shipment volume across cigarettes and smoke free products by 1.4% YoY to 786.5 billion units. This was the 5th consecutive year of volume growth which I am considering a huge achievement from a company that also sells combustible cigarettes in a structurally declining category. PM's smoke free product shipment volumes totaled 179.1 billion units which was up 12.8% YoY as the volume in smoke free pouches increased 18.5% and e-vapor more than doubled. The smoke-free business accounted for 41.5% of total net revenues in 2025 which was up 38.7% YoY and it represented an increase of almost 43% in their total gross profit. PM's smoke-free products are now available in 106 markets and used by an estimated 43 million adult consumers globally which is an increase of roughly 4.5 million people. PM has invested over $16 billion since 2008 in their smoke free division across R&D and commercialization and I believe that it's paying off in spades.

When I look at the regional performance the numbers are enticing. Europe delivered 6.8% organic revenue growth, with adjusted operating income margins expanding to 46.2%. The IQOS HTU adjusted in-market sales grew an estimated 8.6% in 2025 with an acceleration to 10.3% in Q4. Italy returned to double-digit performance while strong growth was delivered in Germany, Greece, Romania, and Spain. Europe surpassed the 50% threshold for net revenues from smoke-free products which is a critical structural milestone. The South and Southeast Asia became a standout margin story as adjusted OI margins increased 350 basis points organically to 34.1%. Revenue grew 6.4% while IQOS HTU adjusted IMS grew an estimated 20.4% in Q4. In East Asia, Australia and the GTR there was 4.6% of organic revenue growth and 300 basis points of organic OI margin expansion to 47.2% Japan is PM's largest IQOS market and adjusted IMS grew an estimated 7.0% for the full year. The Taiwan IQOS launch in Q4 delivered strong results as it penetrated the market capturing 4% market share.

Why PM will continue to gain market share

I thought there was a major notable development from the earnings release as PM is implementing a new organizational model as they have moved from 4 geographic segments to 3 reportable segments. PM will now report on International Smoke-Free, International Combustibles, and U.S which reflects that the smoke-free business is now operating at scale and the U.S. has become a meaningful standalone revenue contributor through ZYN. It looks as if PM's management team wants to provide investors with clearer visibility into the economics of each business unit. I'm viewing this as a positive signal that management is confident enough in the U.S. business to break it out separately and believe that it will help the market better appreciate the standalone value of the ZYN franchise. If I am correct this could help PM get a more favorable rating as the U.S. market opens back up to their business.

In 2025 PM's smoke-free business delivered $16.9 billion in net revenues as it grew 15% YoY. Smoke-free shipment volumes were up 12.8% for the full year while the gross profit surged 20.3% to $11.7 billion. That growth rate materially exceeds the revenue growth rate which demonstrates how powerful the margins of the smoke-free products are. PM is executing on their flywheel as their generating higher volumes to drive manufacturing efficiencies which expand margins and correlate to increased levels of bottom line earnings. I don't think enough people are paying attention to what is happening outside of combustible tobacco because its still considered a taboo industry nut when you look at the business metrics its still extremely profitable.

The Swedish Match acquisition has proven to be a transformative deal as ZYN has become the growth engine in the U.S. for PM. ZYN nicotine pouches have become a juggernaut in the United States as the numbers speak for themselves. U.S. ZYN shipments reached 794 million cans in 2025 which represented a 37% YoY increase. Overall in the Americas region oral SFP shipments totaled 930 million cans which was a 29% increase YoY. Even with the competitive forces from Altria's (MO) on! brand and smaller entrants PM maintained roughly 2/3 of category value share throughout the year at a premium price position.

What makes ZYN's positioning so powerful is the breadth of its consumer sourcing. Management has discussed that ZYN's new users come from cigarettes, vaping, and traditional smokeless products as they each account for roughly a 1/3rd of new adoption. This matters because it means ZYN's addressable market is not limited to a single nicotine category. The pouch category currently represents a high single-digit percentage of total U.S. industry tobacco volumes and the growth trajectory suggests there is a significant runway ahead for PM in the U.S.

IQOS still remains the cornerstone of PM's international smoke-free strategy as the international IQOS HTU shipments grew 11% to 155.1 billion units in 2025. The platform is accounting for roughly 76% of the global heat-not-burn category. IQOS strengthened its overall position as they gained over 1% of combined cigarette and HTU industry volumes in Q4 to reach 9.6% making it the 2nd largest nicotine brand in the markets they operate in. The VEEV which is PM's e-vapor platform doubled its shipment volumes in 2025 and is available in 47 markets. It also holds the #1 volume share position in the closed pods segment in 8 markets. PM reacquired U.S. IQOS commercialization rights and has submitted its IQOS ILUMA application to the FDA. If they become authorized to sell IQOS in the U.S. PM would enter the world's largest smoke-free market with a product that has demonstrated strong conversion rates from combustibles in every market it has entered.

What I'm watching heading into Q1 2026 earnings

PM reports Q1 2026 earnings on April 22nd before the market opens and there are several things I am paying attention to. The Street has the consensus adjusted diluted EPS estimate at $1.82 for Q1 which sits within management's guided range of $1.80 to $1.85. PM has established a track record of beating EPS estimates in 7 of the last 8 quarters and raised guidance 3 times in 2025 so I am expecting them to come in above the top end of estimates. I am also expecting them to reaffirm their 2026 full year guidance on the earnings call. This is a management team that continuously under promises and overdelivers throughout the smoke-free transition and I don't see a reason why this pattern would be disrupted.

This is also the first quarter under PM's new 3-segment reporting structure. They've moved from 4 geographic segments to International Smoke-Free, International Combustibles, and U.S. which means we're finally going to get a clean look at ZYN's standalone profitability. I think that the market is underestimating how much of a tailwind ZYN actually is and seeing this broken out in more detail could get analysts to raise targets. We could also get updates on the IQOS ILUMA application which is still pending and news about ZYN Ultra which is in the FDA's pilot program. Neither of those are baked into the current guidance and if we get positive commentary from management about the regulatory pathway it's likely to act as a catalyst for PM's price action.

I expect PM to deliver a solid quarter and come in above the top end of guidance. 2025 showcased that the underlying business momentum across IQOS internationally and ZYN domestically has allowed the smokeless transition to flourish. The fears about the first round of excise tax increases on heated tobacco products in Japan are overblown in my opinion as this is a known headwind that management has already baked into their outlook. I believe we could see an upside surprise from better-than-expected ZYN shipment trends or a signal that the new U.S. segment is inflecting toward profitability faster than the market expects. When it comes down to it I am expecting a strong quarter from PM that sets the tone for the year with commentary that could create a more bullish narrative based on realistic tailwinds that could increase revenue and profitability.

Philip Morris: An interesting investment

PM has guided for diluted EPS of $7.87 to $8.02 and adjusted diluted EPS of $8.38 to $8.53 which is a growth rate of 11.1% to 13.1% YoY. The key assumptions that PM is making is that they will generate 5% to 7% organic net revenue growth and 7% to 9% organic operating income growth. This should allow them to generate roughly $13.5 billion in operating cash flow. PM has also issued new 2026-2028 compound annual growth targets of 6% to 8% for organic net revenues, 8% to 10% for organic operating income, and 9% to 11% for adjusted diluted EPS excluding currency. PM is on track to deliver at or above the top end of its previous 2024-2026 growth projections which gives me confidence that the 2026-2028 targets are achievable if not conservative.

I think that PM looks inexpensive from an EPS perspective. The Street has the consensus EPS estimates at $8.45 for 2026 and $10.01 in 2028. PM is trading at 18.98 times 2026 earnings and 16.02 times 2028 earnings. I get that they operate in a segment that is unpopular but $1 of cash flow still correlates to $1 of cash flow no matter what segment you operate in. PM is expected to grow their EPS by double digits this year and in the high single digits over the next 2-years. I believe that PM is in an interesting position considering we are seeing a rotation from growth to value.

PM is also paying a dividend of $5.88 per share which is a 3.67% yield. PM is paying out 74.80% of its EPS to shareholders in the form of a dividend which has increased for the past 17 consecutive years. PM's average dividend growth rate is 3.84% over the past 5-years. In addition to the low valuation PM is also paying a dividend that is similar to the risk-free rate of return on short duration assets. I think investors are getting a lot for their capital at these levels.

Risks to investing in Philip Morris

While I am upgrading my investment thesis to bullish on PM there are risk factors to consider. The first is the Japanese excise tax headwinds. Japan is IQOS's largest market and the upcoming excise tax increases in April and October 2026 will raise retail prices by an estimated 10% to 20%. Management said on the earnings call that this creates an atypical year for Japanese volumes and could impact category growth which is a risk to revenue and profitability. There is also regulatory uncertainty as the pending FDA authorization for IQOS ILUMA in the U.S. and ZYN Ultra are not approved yet. While PMI has a strong track record with the FDA any delays or unfavorable outcomes would limit near-term growth in one of the largest markets for nicotine usage. MO also faces competitive products from MO as they have received FDA authorization for their on! nicotine pouches. While ZYN currently has a dominant position this could change in the future. There is also a health risk that young adults adopt a healthier lifestyle and choose not to use PM's products which would hurt their organic growth plans. Investors should do their own research because there are certainly many reasons that the investment thesis could be impacted.

Conclusion

I am upgrading my investment thesis back to bullish as PM is not the tobacco company your grandfather owned. This is a company executing a generational business model transformation. The smoke-free business has crossed the 40% revenue threshold and ZYN has become the dominant U.S. nicotine pouch brand with 794 million cans shipped. IQOS maintains approximately 76% of global heat-not-burn share and PM is working on their US authorizations. I think that PM is trading at an attractive valuation at under 19 times earnings with a dividend yield that is around 3.67%. As we see the rotation from growth to value I think that PM is a name that will resonate with value investors especially as the dividend continues to grow on an annualized basis.

data.title: Oracle: The Most Underrated AI Infrastructure Play
data.description:
The bull thesis of Oracle Corporation (ORCL) is at that inflection point where the growth of cloud infrastructure is rapidly increasing due to the irrepressible demand for AI compute. The company's latest quarterly results have also proved this. In Q3, obligations (RPO) grew to more than half a trillion, offering the company multi-year visibility of its revenue, which is uncommon in enterprise technology.

However, Oracle continues to burn its cash with historically high capital expenditure and negative free cash flow, which indicates that the fundamental business model the company is still in the process of converting business to customer-financed infrastructure.

Even though ORCL has soared over the past year and since pulled back, Oracle can build a dedicated AI cloud ecosystem that can help it provide quality and sustainable growth throughout the rest of the year.

The most important thing is that after the sharp pullback of 25% down in 2026, the stock becomes cheap. It's trading at around 20x forward earnings below the sector median of 22x. So I believe with the strong demand for AI and computing, plus the strong backlog, the company will generate tons of revenue from business, which means the stock is ready to rebound from this slump.

$553 Billion Backlog and 84% IaaS Growth Drive a $90 Billion Revenue Goal

I believe that Oracle has attained a significant operational edge when placing Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) as a leading choice for AI training and inference workloads. Oracle generated top-line amounts of $17.2 billion during Q3 FY26, which is 22% higher than it was last year. OCI was the star in the spotlight, and the revenue of infrastructure-as-a-service (IaaS) increased with a mind-boggling growth of 84% to $4.9 billion. This is a massive improvement as compared with the 68% increase in the previous quarter, which shows that there is not only a high demand for AI capacity, but it is also driving hard. Cloud apps (SaaS) were up 13% to a total of $4 billion, and total cloud revenue (SaaS plus IaaS) increased 44% to $8.9 billion; this is 52% of Oracle's total business now.

Oracle has a record backlog of pending performance obligations of $553 billion. This amount is a 325% year-over-year and sequential growth of $29 billion. This backlog has been largely made up of huge-scale AI contracts on which the customers have subscribed to long-term compute capacity. Importantly, the capital intensity of Oracle is changing the structure of these deals. The management revealed that this RPO rise has been in the form of contracts, with most equipment being financed in advance, either through prepayments by the customers or sold to the customers directly. This prepay, and bring-your-own-hardware model also works to the advantage of Oracle insofar that it no longer has to generate incremental capital to finance these particular deals, shifting their revenue increase off their cash flow burden.

Additionally, the backlog and the increasing growth of IaaS contribute to the fact that the future outlook of top-line is solid, and, in 2027, the management will boost its fiscal 2027 revenue projections to $90 billion. I find that the capability of Oracle to sell Nvidia graphics cards as well as other chips reduces the cost of computing among clients and enhances the resiliency of the supply chain. Oracle stated it would roll out 50,000 AMD MI450 AI accelerators beginning in the second half of 2026, in October 2025, providing its customers a feasible substitute to AI inference workloads.

All in all, this diversification positions Oracle to win over spending on companies expanding AI operations, and they may get superior pricing and opportunities relative to their competitors, who depend on one vendor.

On the other hand, internal efficiencies are also increasing with the introduction of AI tools. Oracle used over 1,000 embedded AI agents in its Fusion applications, and its AI code generation technology allowed Oracle to develop more applications using less manpower. This increases its SaaS suites within additional industries at reduced cost that safeguards margins. The notable competitive achievements in the course of Q3 were Memorial Hermann Health System, Investec Bank, and a large Wall Street bank, which all shifted massive loads of work to Oracle Fusion Cloud instead of rivals.

Therefore, the collaboration between the advanced cloud-based infrastructure, other silicon access, and the creation of software as a service is a potent unit working as a revenue generator on various scales. This integration helps to sustain high-margin recurring top line and secure OCI market share against bigger hyperscalers such as Amazon (AMZN) AWS and Microsoft (MSFT) Azure. Non-GAAP operating margin of the company entered a healthy high of up to 43% until Q3, and this highlights the underlying profitability of this model.

At that, Oracle's strategic moves do not stop here at pure infrastructure. The company also just became a 15% equity investor in the newly independent TikTok U.S., with a seat on the board of directors, and became the sole technology provider on the platform. Oracle is already building AI content algorithms. That lets it capture the computing costs behind them. This would diversify Oracle’s revenue even more.

Furthermore, Oracle emphasizes that its Sovereign AI clouds, where localized data centers are designed to serve national governments, which are benefiting in a market where high barriers to data residency pose a significant barrier to entry for other competitors in the market.

Moving towards the balance sheet, despite the quarterly negative Cash flow available from operations, it has been positive at the trailing twelve months, reaching towards $23.5 billion, 13% year-over-year, and will provide significant internal funding even at the current massive investment cycle. Plus, the free cash flow of the legacy software and support business software revenues contributed $6.1 billion in Q3, which still supports the aggressive cloud expansion at a rate not yet disruptive to core operations.

Oracle’s $50 Billion CapEx Burns Cash, Risking Negative FCF Through FY29

A massive capital expenditure plan is a huge problem for the financial position of Oracle. As we know, Oracle planned to go to CapEx of $50 billion in FY26. So the effect on the free cash flow has been quite terrible. For instance, in Q3 FY26, the capital expenditures were at an enormous level of $12.8 billion in the quarter, much higher than in the previous quarter. As a result, the trailing twelve months of the free cash flow dropped to negative $12.5 billion. That was a drastic decline compared to the positive figures only a year ago.

Plus, the analysts' forecasts show that Oracle is going to keep burning money for a number of years, though we dont know how many.

According to an analyst at Jefferies, Brent Thill, it is not anticipated that free cash flow will become positive until FY29, i.e., that Oracle will need to finance itself in the interim. This already happened a few months ago, when Oracle told investors it needed to raise between $45 billion and $50 billion, some from borrowing, some from selling shares.

In the result, we have seen earlier in March, in a mix of investment-grade bonds and mandatory convertible preferred stock, the company raised as much as $30 billion, with the order book being massively oversubscribed. Nevertheless, this issue of billions of new debt increases interest payments, which puts a heavy strain on the balance sheet. As of now, the total notes payable, as well as borrowings, were approximately $124 billion.

Additionally, the speed of the development of AI infrastructure is associated with depreciation accounting risks. The Street is suggesting that modern GPUs can become economically obsolete in a few years, while depreciation schedules assume a longer life. In case the physical hardware becomes obsolete at a faster rate than the accounting schedules require, Oracle might be required to perform drastic write-downs in the future, which might affect long-term profitability.

And one more thing. The queue appears massive on paper. However, regarding a regulatory submission, the percentage of that backlog that will become revenue in the next twelve months is only a small percentage. The majority of it will not be evident in a few years. Thus, the cash flow of the backlog will fall a long way behind the expenditure on infrastructure. I am concerned about that timing discrepancy.

In my opinion, Oracle is putting money on AI. However, the economic suffering is a fact, and it is occurring in the present day.

Oracle’s Valuation Looks Fair, Not Cheap

The valuation of Oracle is the one part that today investors like the most. At the current level, the shares appeared more like a fair-growth stock than a bargain, with 20 times trailing earnings and 6.67 times sales. That would make it more valuable than a peer group of Microsoft (MSFT), Salesforce (CRM), and SAP (SAP), which trade at 22.31, 22.96, and 23.05 times earnings, and 8.68, 4.33, and 4.59 times sales, respectively.

Oracle is approximately 10% higher than the peer average on earnings and about 14% higher than the peer average on sales, and this is not a cheap stock, even following its recent decline. Oracle would have to have about 10% more profits at such a price to justify an average P/E with its peers. A factor of 14% more revenue would be required to support a peer-average P/S.

The positive thing is that the math of Wall Street growth is good enough to back that argument. Analysts see revenue of up to $88 billion in fiscal 2027, and the company itself indicated the goal of attaining $90 billion, against a fiscal 2026 forecast of $67 billion, which would be approximately 29 to 34% growth.

That puts ORCL at a moderately good price in the event that management performs, yet somewhat costly in the event that the AI cloud boom fades.

The Bottom line

The greatest competitive edge of Oracle is its special AI infrastructure that uses a record backlog of $553 billion, access to different chips, and customer-financed implementation to create customer value at every level of demand. Although the heavy spending of CapEx pressure on cash flow is pushing the company toward high levels of debt and equity capital, the business model of high margins under development will yield billions in yearly revenues by FY27. But if only the execution is good. Because the present cash burn creates a sustainable moat in the AI cloud wars for investors who have a multi-year horizon.

data.title: Red Cat Holdings: The Setup Just Got Better
data.description:
Red Cat Holdings, Inc. (RCAT) is up around 8% in premarket trading on Wednesday, moving mainly in sympathy with the overall market recovery on the ceasefire talks between the U.S. and Iran.

The stock has been through it over the past month, down over 20%, and over the past 6 months, down another 18%. Still, year to date, the stock is up around 52%. I last covered Red Cat in mid September of last year, and saw more upside following the inclusion of the Black Widow drone in the NATO NSPA catalog, maintaining my buy from mid-August, with the stock up over 43.6% since then, outperforming the market, with the S&P 500 (SP500) up 2.5% during the same period.

I called RCAT “my favorite drone stock this year” back in 2025, and I think that worked out well in retrospect, and I’m doubling down this year. I remain convinced there’s more upside ahead for the stock, and think the dip over the past month and 6 months is worth buying.

Back in September, I said that

“Red Cat offers a medium-risk, high-reward opportunity with some very nice contracts and accelerator catalysts over the next 12 to 24 months.”

That said, today's ceasefire-driven move is largely noise for my long-term investment thesis. This is not a defense stock that benefits from de-escalation. The company benefits from the structural, multi-year shift in how Western militaries procure and deploy unmanned systems, and that trend does not reverse on a two-week pause in the Iran war. So, let's separate the premarket jump from the investment thesis, since the latter has gotten materially stronger since my September note.

What has changed since September

The news flow since my last coverage has been dense, so let’s jump right in. The company reported Q4 '25 on the 18th of March, and the stock dipped around 22% in the 2 trading days that followed. Revenue for the quarter came in at $26.2 million, up 1,985% year over year and 172% sequentially, and beat consensus by $2.31 million, while EPS came in at -$0.17, missing consensus by $0.02.

Full-year 2025 revenue hit $40.7 million, up 161% year over year from $15.6 million. Production capacity grew 520% to 254,000 square feet. Cash ended the year at $167.9 million, up from $9.2 million in a year earlier, supported by equity issuance, and the balance sheet concern that shadowed the stock in 2024 is no longer the primary risk.

In December 2025, a second Asia-Pacific ally ordered Black Widow systems via competitive tender, the second such international military customer for the platform, and in March of this year, a NATO ally selected Black Widow through a competitive NSPA tender, with deliveries scheduled across 2026. Each system includes two aircraft and a ground control station. According to the company’s CEO Jeff Thompson,

This contract reflects a broader shift toward systems that can move quickly from procurement to deployment…NATO allies need platforms that are deployable today for use in contested environments, built on secure U.S. supply chains, and able to be manufactured and fielded at scale. Black Widow is designed for that reality, delivering reliable ISR at the tactical edge where speed, readiness, and electronic warfare resilience are critical to mission success.

Also in early March, Allen Control Systems joined Red Cat's Futures Initiative to advance autonomous counter-drone and precision defense capabilities. According to Mike Wior, co-founder and CEO, ACS,

U.S. and allied nations are seeking cost-effective and scalable counter-drone systems to provide a new layer of protection on the move and at the edge…Partnering with Red Cat and joining the Futures Initiative allows us to integrate our fully autonomous precision weapons station into a proven unmanned platform, advancing these systems beyond basic requirements and enabling them to execute more advanced and complex missions for U.S. military forces and their allies.

That is a meaningful sequence of catalysts in under three weeks. The story has moved from an interesting drone company with a promising Army contract to a company actively building international revenue, swarm autonomy, and maritime systems through Blue Ops. According to Thompson on the earnings call,

Blue Ops is a strategic and important part of Red Cat's family of systems. It opens the rest of the globe for Red Cat. Our family of systems was limited to 30% of earth. With our new Variant 7 and other hulls, we can now launch from 100% of the globe. We believe Blue Ops could be very helpful in Venezuela, in the USVIs, the Gulf of America, Cuba and urgently in the Strait of Hormuz.

On the latter, he added that management thinks their presence in the Middle East is a long-term tailwind, as they

“believe that the Strait is going to need to be protected for not years, forever from now on. No longer is it going to be acceptable for the world to be held hostage by that one gap in small area there between the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman. So we think this is a very long-term solution is to not have billion ships escorting tankers.”

Valuation:

Every earnings-based multiple, including P/E, EV/EBITDA, EV/EBIT, and Price/Cash Flow, is not meaningful because there are no earnings to divide by. On that basis, the premium was severe and remains so at current levels. EV/Sales TTM is 32.26x, compared with a sector median of 2.21x. Price/Sales TTM is 29.35x versus a sector median of 1.85x. Even on a forward basis, where the market is implicitly assuming a major revenue step-up in 2026, EV/Sales drops to 8.83x and Price/Sales to 9.83x, still four to five times the sector median. Red Cat is a pure growth bet, and the market is paying well above sector rates for the optionality.

The stock is currently set to open at around $13, down from $18.78 all time high back in early March. While I’m not necessarily calling the bottom here, as more downside is always on the table, I think the risk-reward is favorable at current levels.

The stock is trading below its EMA21 and EMA50 at $13.7 and $13.4, respectively, with both acting as near-term resistance, while the EMA200 at $10.9 remains long-term support. With RSI flatlining at 43, I would like to see it recover to the mid-50s to 60s range and the stock hold above its resistance EMAs.

Risks and what’s next:

Net loss for the quarter widened to $72.1 million in 2025 from $53.5 million.

Net cash used in operations was $89.1 million, up from $25 million in a year ago. Revenue growth is exceptional, but profitability is not approaching. Gross margin for the full year was 3.1%, and even in the record Q4, it only reached 4.2%, meaning the company is barely covering the cost of goods sold on the revenue they're generating.

The cash position looks healthy at $167.9 million, but that was built almost entirely through equity issuance ($234.3 million raised), not through the business generating cash. The company is scaling aggressively on equity-financed capital, and if contract delivery timelines slip or the Army program volumes disappoint, the multiple could compress quickly.

I called it my favorite drone stock in 2025, and I'm not walking that back. The next twelve months have more hard catalysts than the prior twelve, and they include international deliveries, Blue Ops' first USV prototypes, and a May earnings print that should show Q1 revenue continuing the Q4 momentum. The risk profile has not improved because the company remains a pre-profitability, high-burn, high-multiple small cap in a sector where sentiment can swing violently, but the reward profile has, if anything, expanded, backed by more product lines, more international customers, and more strategic partnerships.

The thesis is intact, and I see this dip as a buying opportunity. My bullish take on the stock doesn’t require profitability today, just a credible line of sight to it, and, at current levels, I think we have that.

data.title: Enphase: Do Not Bottom-Fish Here - More Recovery Headwinds Ahead
data.description:
I previously reiterated my Hold rating for Enphase Energy, Inc. (ENPH) in December 2025, attributed to the management's underwhelming FQ4'25/FQ1'26 guidance and the growing regulatory/demand headwinds.

In this article, I shall discuss my reiterated Hold rating, attributed to ENPH's likely to remain underwhelming recovery prospects in the US and the EU, pending further clarity in the upcoming FQ1'26 earnings call.

ENPH Faces Numerous Recovery Headwinds

Since my prior Hold rating, ENPH has charted an outsized recovery to hit a new high of $51s after the well-received FQ4'25 earnings call, albeit with the stock quickly reversing in momentum to retest the $33s by the time of writing.

It is apparent from these developments that the market's exuberance about ENPH's supposedly bottoming FQ1'26 prospects and the potentially sequentially growing FQ2'26 demand profile does not last.

1. FQ1'26 Preview

With ENPH set to report their upcoming FQ1'26 performance on April 28, 2026 (Post-Market), I urge readers to compare their upcoming performance to the management's previous FQ1'26 guidance of:

  • Revenues at $285M at the midpoint (-16.9% QoQ/-19.9% YoY),
  • IQ Batteries shipments at 110 MWh at the midpoint (-26.6% QoQ/-35.3% YoY), and
  • Adjusted gross margins of 43.5% (-2.6 points QoQ/-5.4 points YoY),

Since these numbers have yet to inspire confidence surrounding their uncertain demand recovery, their top/bottom line performance is likely to remain lumpy throughout the turbulent 2026.

This is especially since another energy storage peer, Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), has reported their notably lower energy storage deployment of 8.8 GWh in FQ1'26 (-38% QoQ/-15.3% YoY).

This is on top of market sentiments seemingly shifting to stocks offering rapidly deployed, on-site energy generation offerings, such as Bloom Energy Corporation (BE), and longer-term nuclear/gas-related utility stocks, since “power availability is not keeping pace with digital infrastructure demand.”

This development is unsurprising indeed, given that S&P Global already expects the US data center power consumption to grow from 57.68 GW in 2025 to 103.53 GW in 2030, attributed to the accelerated data center build-outs by the top few hyperscalers/neoclouds over the next few years.

The demand headwind for solar/battery storage may also be why the consensus has priced in a relatively lower FQ1'26 revenue estimate of $282.62M (-20.6% YoY) against ENPH's FQ1'26 revenue guidance of $285M at the midpoint (-19.9% YoY), with it hinting at the consensus' relatively low expectations entering FY2026.

2. Regulatory Policy & Intensifying Competition

Readers may also want to note that the current regulatory climate appears to be unfavorable towards solar and EV offerings, as similarly observed in:

  • The Solar Energy ​Industries Association reporting a lower US solar installation at 43 GW in 2025 (-14% YoY).
  • Cox Automotive reporting a lower new BEV registration by -28% YoY in the US for Q1'26.

The potentially lower FY2026 numbers are triggering headwinds for ENPH's solar inverter and EV charging opportunities within the US, as observed in the flattish US-based revenues at $304M in FQ4'25 (inline YoY).

With FQ4'25 already benefiting from the “increased solar and battery installations ahead of the Section 25D tax credit expiration,” readers may want to temper their near-term expectations, since the pull-forward sales cadence in FQ4'25 may trigger an underwhelming US performance in FQ1'26.

While ENPH has previously highlighted their robust opportunities in the EU, their sales tailwinds have yet to be translated into the currently impacted FQ4'25 EU sales of $39M (-51.8% YoY).

With EU developments “remaining heavily dependent on Chinese battery suppliers” for “reliable, cost-effective, and proven products,” it is unsurprising that ENPH has reported “pricing pressure” in the prior earnings call, with their gross margins likely to deteriorate from FQ1'26 onwards as well.

Pending further demand recovery signals through the management's upcoming commentaries and the FQ2'26 guidance in the FQ1'26 earnings call, I am of the opinion that ENPH's recent momentum reversal is likely to continue for a little longer despite the stock already losing -34.8% of its value after the February 2026 top.

3. Discounted Valuations Meet Resilience

Combined with the recent profit-taking after the well-received FQ4'25 earnings call, it is unsurprising that ENPH has been corrected to FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 15.92x by the time of writing, down from the recent highs of 23.19x, the 5Y mean of 42.74x, and the sector median of 21.63x.

Even then, given the flattish adjusted EPS prospects between the FY2025 performance at $2.96 and the consensus FY2028 estimates of $3.13, I am of the opinion that ENPH is likely to underperform expectations in the intermediate term.

It is apparent from these headwinds that the stock is only suitable for those with a longer investing trajectory and a higher risk appetite, given their uncertain reversal prospects.

The silver lining to ENPH's investment thesis will be its still healthy balance sheet at a net cash position of $0.3B in FY2025 (in line YoY), aided by the recent sale of advanced manufacturing production tax credits, which is likely to temper some near-term risks from the lumpy consumer demand.

This is especially since the company remains profitable on a free cash flow basis at $95.9M in FY2025 (-80% YoY), despite the notable YoY deterioration, thanks to their asset-light strategy from their reliance on contract manufacturers across the US (to benefit from the regulatory credit) and China/India (to benefit from the lower COGS).

ENPH Remains A Hold

For now, ENPH's 7Y stock price chart appears alarming indeed, with the stock returning all of its prior 2020-2022 gains, and it remains to be seen if the bulls may defend the November 2025 bottom of $26s.

Given the mix of pessimistic headlines across TSLA's lower energy storage demand, the ongoing regulatory headwinds in the US, and the demand/pricing headwinds in the EU, I am of the opinion that ENPH is likely to remain oversold in the near term, pending further clarity in the FQ1'26 earnings call sometime in late April 2026.

Based on the twice-confirmed FWD P/E floor of ~12x in August/November 2025 and the consensus FY2028 adjusted EPS estimates of $3.13, ENPH may continue trading sideways along my base-case long-term price target of $37s, with it presenting its underwhelming return prospects at current levels.

Pending a more permissive regulatory landscape, the return in retail/utility demand for solar/EV offerings, and the improvements in its top/bottom lines, I believe that ENPH is likely to underperform in the intermediate term, resulting in my reiterated Hold rating.

Do not bottom-fish here.

data.title: SCHD: Still My Gold Standard
data.description:
The start to 2026 has certainly been more exciting than many expected. While the private credit drama was beginning to heat up well before the beginning of the year, the introduction of new kinetic conflict in the middle east combined with resurging inflation and worries around economic have jarred investor confidence.

Year to date, the S&P 500 is down around 4%, despite and impressive rally last week in the face of a deteriorating global situation.

Typically, when the going gets tough for global markets, I encourage folks to look outside of their typical core and find noncorrelated asset classes that might be undervalued. Well, private credit and business development companies look anything but safe these days. REITs continue to struggle while they refinance into higher interest rates and continue to digest pocketed issues of oversupply. The specter of higher interest rates continues to haunt the idea of buying into longer duration bonds.

This had led investors to seek out more conservative options, but even treasuries are down materially in the past six months.

I still see another opportunity despite an ongoing sector rotation. I believe dividend stocks look increasingly attractive against the current investment backdrop. This article will discuss why and explain why the Schwab Equity Dividend ETF (SCHD) is still my dividend fund of choice.

Review of Prior Coverage

SCHD is one of my most covered funds on Seeking Alpha. Historically, it has been my heaviest dividend ETF allocation, followed closely by the Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM). If you look at its performance against peers and the S&P 500, it’s not difficult to see why I like it.

I usually cover the fund on around an annual basis. My last coverage of the fund was in March 2025 when we covered how dividend stocks protect against market volatility. This topic ended up being timely as the nation began throwing tariffs around the world shortly thereafter.

Hard to believe it’s already been a year. This prior covearge Of the fund included an overview of the portfolio and the corresponding index.

SCHD is an index ETF sponsored by Charles Schwab (SCHW). Among SCHW’s lineup of low cost ETFs, SCHD is an all-star and one of the largest dividend ETFs, overshadowed only by a select few such as the Vanguard High Yield Dividend ETF (VYM).

…SCHD continues to be one of the few ETFs to follow the Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 index. The Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 index is an index of 100 dividend paying companies that are located in the United States. An overview is provided below.

“Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 Index. Measures the stock performance of 100 high dividend paying U.S. companies with a record of consistently paying dividends, selected for fundamental strength relative to their peers, based on financial ratios.”

As an index fund, I constantly reiterate that SCHD follows a passive management style. This means stock selection is not up to the discretion of the manager. My historical coverage of the fund has done a deep description of the criteria that the fund uses to select its investments. This includes prioritizing debt metrics and free cash flow to assess dividend health and growth.

Since publication of my prior coverage of SCHD, the fund has performed well. Stacked up against the S&P 500, the fund has kept pace, supported by a jump this year. This also includes the remarkable tech resurgence that took place shortly after the tariff madness. More recently, SCHD has begun to pull ahead against the broader indexes.

Year to date, the fund has outperformed the S&P 500.

Recent Reconstitution

SCHD is an actively managed fund, but that does not mean that the portfolio does not change. Following the passive investment style, the portfolio is periodically reconstituted rebalancing and replacing certain assets in order to once again meet the quantitative methodology behind the underlying index.

This means the top 10 holdings of the fund look different on an annual basis. Some notable names were removed from the fund during the March reconstitution. This includes some large names including AbbVie (ABBV), Cisco (CSCO), and Valero (VLO). ABBV was removed despite being a dividend king. This could partially be attributed to underlying financial metrics such as declining free cash flow to total debt. If you were to look past towards the core business, cash flow is also coming under pressure through changing economics like the TrumpRx platform. In this case, drugs like Humira are now selling at deep disounts.

AbbVie’s mega-blockbuster Humira will be sold through the government’s direct-to-consumer (DTC) platform TrumpRx for around $950, roughly an 86% discount to its usual price, which can reach over $6,900 for uninsured patients, CBS Newsreported on Tuesday.

In another instance, CSCO was removed. Most likely this is due to declining dividend growth and the company’s increasing spending which puts pressure on free cash flow and debt ratios.

Looking from a sector perspective, exposure to energy was reduced by around 8% on the heels of extraordinary success over the past several months due to geopolitical change. Much of the exposure was replaced with the fund investing more heavily in healthcare, including the addition of UnitedHealth Group (UNH), which accounts for nearly 4% of assets. According to Seeking Alpha, healthcare is the largest sector allocation for the fund, representing nearly 19% of total assets.

Reconstitutions are important for a fund like SCHD, but it’s even more advantageous for its investors. SCHD’s rotation has historically Positioned the fund to buy the winners while selling the losers. This means capital is continually rotating between sectors without shareholders of SCHD even knowing it. However, more importantly, this is also done in a tax deferred format. In contrast to a mutual fund, capital gains are not realized by the investor when SCHD makes changes to the portfolio.

Valuation Concerns

Over the past several years, I have highlighted dividend stocks as an attractive opportunity for several reasons. First and foremost, the valuation of some of the largest companies in the United States has become slightly outrageous. Although the scene has begun to change already, some of the largest companies including Apple (AAPL) and Microsoft (MSFT) still trade at valuations that are significantly richer than 10 years ago.

Believe it or not, AAPL had a low double digit earnings multiple assigned to it roughly a decade ago, a star contrast to the >30x multiple that the company currently enjoys. Microsoft now trades at 22x earnings after dropping more than 30% from all time highs less than six months ago.

The bigger they are, the harder they fall. I recently published an article explaining more of the dangers around extreme valuations in detail. Don’t let me convince you, let Howard Marks of Oaktree do a better job with his memo, The Calculus of Value, published last year.

He touches on a rather shocking statistic that I hold in my back pocket…When the S&P 500 has traded at 23x forward earnings, returns have never been great.

At the time, J.P. Morgan published a graph showing that if you bought the S&P 500 index at 23 times the coming year’s earnings per share in the period 1987-2014 (the only period for which there’s data on forward-looking p/e ratios and resulting ten-year returns), your average annual return over the subsequent ten years was between plus 2% and minus 2% every time. To the extent this p/e ratio history is relevant, it bodes pretty poorly for the S&P 500.

Put graphically, this actually begins to look even worse.

According to JP Morgan (JPM), the current forward PE multiple of the S&P 500 is around 22x, around five turns higher than the 30 year average of 17x.

This all highlights the risks associated with investing at extreme valuations. We can breathe a sigh of relief when we look into SCHD and realize the fund’s largest new additions are trading at more attractive valuations. For example, UNH is trading at under 16x earnings. Similarly, the fund has rotated into some all stars in challenged sectors, like Blackstone (BX) who has been feeling the private credit burn, and trades at 18x earnings.

As a dividend fund should, SCHD offers a breathe of fresh air from the S&P 500 with no exposure to the largest tech names like AAPL and MSFT.

Dividends

SCHD’s dividend growth track record has been outstanding against other dividend funds. The fund’s annual distribution has increased by 6% and 10% over the last three and ten years, respectively. This doubles the ten year growth rate of the S&P 500 with a much higher current yield, even ten years ago.

The fund has also more than doubled the dividend growth rate of VYM over the past ten years.

In the fund’s report on the annual reconstitution, data indicates that the historical dividend growth rate of the funds holdings has increased by around 75 basis points post reconstitution. All the while, the fund gives up just two basis points of current dividend yield. Return on equity and free cash flow as a portion of debt have both increased as well. Take this with a grain of salt given these are backward looking numbers.

Conclusion

As the market continues to run away with expanding valuations, a flight to quality has already begun. After a tepid year, dividend stocks have once again stepped into the spotlight as an attractive opportunity. In the past, I have talked about the import buoy effect that dividends can provide. In this case, SCHD’s recent reconstitution provides a fresh look for the top ten holdings and continues to invest in reasonably valued businesses that are financially well positioned.

In the meantime, the yield offered by SCHD is materially higher than broader stock indices like the S&P 500, and are on par with most REITs and their funds, including the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ).

SCHD continues to earn a Buy rating as my gold standard of dividend ETFs.

data.title: AbbVie: Upside Potential And Dividends With Competent Portfolio Renewal
data.description:
I previously covered AbbVie Inc. (ABBV) (ABBV:CA) in October 2024, discussing why the stock remained a great Buy albeit upon a slight correction to the Q1'24 levels of $180s for an improved upside potential.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for ABBV as the stock nears its Q4'25/Q1'26 technical support, aided by the management's excellent portfolio renewal beyond the prior Humira patent expiry.

ABBV Proves Their Big Pharma Competency

Since my last Buy rating nearer to the Q1'24 levels of $180s for an improved margin of safety, ABBV has already pulled back as posited, albeit more than expected to the prior trading floor of $160s after the poorly received FQ3'24 earnings call.

1. Portfolio Renewal

Otherwise, given the established uptrend support line since November 2023, it is apparent that the pharmaceutical company remains well positioned to drive renewed growth beyond Humira's prior patent expiry in 2023, thanks to Skyrizi's and Rinvoq's promising double-digit growth thus far.

For example, ABBV has reported a robust global Skyrizi net revenues of $5B in FQ4'25 (+32.5% YoY) and global Rinvoq net revenues of $2.374B (+29.5% YoY), with it well balancing the ongoing Humira erosion at $1.246B (-25.9% YoY) to deliver an excellent overall revenues of $16.618B (+10% YoY).

This alone presents the most important picture of ABBV's pharmaceutical capability, significantly aided by Skyrizi's and Rinvoq's elongated patent expiry through 2033 and 2037, with it underscoring their resilient portfolio prospects.

Readers may also want to note that Skyrizi (risankizumab) is a result of ABBV's global collaboration with Boehringer Ingelheim since 2016, with the initial US FDA approval for psoriasis in 2019 and the subsequent indication expansion to active ulcerative colitis in 2024 underscoring the management's success in pushing out blockbuster therapies.

This is significantly aided by the Skyrizi's expanding prescription share for the US Biologic Psoriasis market at over 45%, Psoriatic Arthritis at 25%, and Inflammatory Bowel Disease at 75%, thanks to their "simple quarterly dosing."

This is on top of ABBV's competent in-house R&D efforts, with Rinvoq being developed internally and the prior settlement with generic manufacturers prolonging the therapy's patent expiry from the previous number of 2033 to 2037 instead.

With Rinvoq already demonstrating doubled remission rates against other treatments, it is unsurprising that the management already expects to capture "incremental share capture" for Rheumatoid Arthritis over the next few years.

Particularly, readers may want to note that ABBV's R&D efforts are rather efficient at 14.8% of its FY2025 revenues (-7.9 points YoY), compared to other pharmaceutical peers similarly facing patent cliff issues, including:

  • Vyndaqel family/Eliquis/Prevnar family (2025/2026 expiry) for Pfizer Inc. (PFE) at 16.6% in FY2025,
  • Keytruda (2028 expiry) for Merck (MRK) at 24.2% in FY2025,
  • Opdivo/Keytruda (2028 expiry) for Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY) at 20.6% in FY2025, and
  • Wegovy (2026 expiry, in certain countries) for Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) (NONOF) at 16.8% in FY2025,

with it lending credibility to the management's competence thus far.

Combined with the Skyrizi/Rinvoq revenues at $7.374B in FQ4'25 (+31.3% YoY) and $25.86B in FY2025 (+46.2% YoY), it is unsurprising that ABBV has offered the promising Skyrizi/Rinvoq revenue guidance of over $31B in FY2026 (+19.8% YoY).

These numbers notably exceed the management's original "guidance for combined revenues of more than $27 billion in 2027" and Humira's peak sales performance of $22B.

2. Lowered FY2026 Guidance

With ABBV set to report their upcoming FQ1'26 earnings call on April 29, 2026, I urge readers to temper their expectations entering the call, since the management has notably lowered their FY2026 adj EPS guidance to $14.06 at the midpoint (+40.6% YoY).

This is down from the prior number of $14.47 at the midpoint (+44.7% YoY) offered in the FQ4'25 earnings call.

Otherwise, it is important to note that ABBV's lowered FY2026 guidance is merely attributed to the $0.41 impact from the Acquired IPR&D and Milestones Expense in FQ1'26, barring which, the adjusted sum remains stable at ~$14.47.

If anything, the same has been observed in their prior FY2025 performance at $10, or more accurately, $12.76 (+9.6% YoY, after including a $2.76 Acquired IPR&D and Milestones Expense).

If anything, readers may want to note that the consensus have quietly raised their forward estimates, with ABBV expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line growth prospects at a CAGR of +8.3%/+21% through FY2028.

This is compared to the historical growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +6%/-1.1% and a 10Y CAGR of +10.4%/+8.8%, respectively, as their newly acquired/in-house pipelines drive their renewed growth prospects beyond Humira's prior patent expiry in 2022.

3. Compellingly Valued, Renewed Growth Prospects

Thanks to the -15.5% correction from the 52 week highs and the raised consensus forward estimates, it is unsurprising that ABBV is a lot more compelling at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 14.38x, down from the recent highs of 19.70x and the sector median of 17.24x while nearing the 5Y mean of 13.74x and 10Y mean of 12.06x.

Based on the cheaper FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 14.38x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +21%, it is apparent that ABBV remains compelling at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.68x, compared to the sector median of 1.66x and the 10Y mean of 1.37x.

Thanks to the ongoing renewal, I am of the opinion that ABBV's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 14.38x and my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.68x remains compelling against some of its pharmaceutical peers facing a similar patent cliff risk, including:

  • PFE at 9.57x/NA,
  • MRK at 23.59x/3.57x,
  • BMY at 9.50x/NA, and
  • NVO at 11.03x/NA, respectively,

with it perhaps explaining why ABBV remains well defended at the $200s since September 2025, despite the recent correction from the Q4'25/Q1'26 highs.

This is especially since ABBV continues to report a relatively healthy balance sheet at a net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of 2.68x in FY2025 (compared to FY2019 levels of 1.78x), as similarly observed in its more than decent Interest Coverage (TTM) at 7.30x (compared to the sector median of 6.87x).

This development implies their ability to sustain their in-house R&D and M&A acquisitions in the near-term, aided by the accretive impact on the therapies' future monetization prospects.

ABBV Is An Even Better Buy After The Dip

These reasons may also be why ABBV has remained well supported along the two uptrend support line since the March 2020 bottom, with the recent sideways trading merely attributed to the stock growing into its previously premium valuations.

This is especially since the stock is now trading nearer to my fair value estimates of $183.40, based on:

  • the FY2025 adj EPS performance at $12.76 (+9.6% YoY, including a $2.76 Acquired IPR&D and Milestones Expense) and
  • the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 14.38x (cheap against the sector median of 17.24x).

Based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $17.71, it goes without saying there is an expanded upside potential:

  • of +23.1% to my long-term, price target of $254.60,
  • if not, of +45.6% to my bull-case long-term price target of $301.00, assuming an upward re-rating nearer to the sector P/E median of ~17x.

Combined with the relatively stable forward dividend yield at 3.31% (compared to the 5Y mean of 3.79% and richer than the sector median of 1.61%) despite the 5Y stock price gains by +96.1%, it is apparent that its investment thesis remains compelling.

ABBV remains a Great Buy here, aided by the recent dip.

Risk Warning

As discussed in segment 2, readers may want to temper their adj EPS expectations moving forward, since ABBV's ongoing portfolio renewal is likely to trigger near-term noise in their bottom-line performance.

Otherwise, given that the ongoing M&A activities have been accretive to their growth prospects, I urge readers to focus on their adjusted numbers for an improved clarity to their quarterly performance.

At the same time, with ABBV set to offer Humira on a steep -86% discount on the TrumpRx platform, I am of the opinion that the therapy's top-line erosion is likely to worsen entering FY2026 while putting further pressure to Skyrizi's/Rinvoq's top-line driver capabilities.

While the TrumpRx deal is expected to last only for three years, I urge readers to closely monitor their execution, since it remains to be seen if more therapies may be added to the list and thus, trigger further top-line erosion risks.

data.title: 5 Markets Getting Pummelled Right Now
data.description:
Equities should see a large rally on Wednesday thanks to an 11th hour two-week ceasefire with Iran reached after the bell on Tuesday. This should help reopen the Strait of Hormuz, at least temporarily, and trigger a big rally in stocks and a big decline in oil prices. Hopefully, a permanent resolution to these hostilities can be reached in short order.

Overall equity valuations remain stretched as stocks have been quite resilient despite the spike in volatility over the last month and a half. The S&P 500 began Wednesday, just 5.5% off from its all-time highs. However, under the surface, there are many markets and areas of the economy that are getting pummeled. Wednesday's relief rally is not going to change that picture except very marginally.

In today's article, we examine five of these problem areas in more detail. And even if the conflict with Iran finds a permanent offramp in the near future, these problems will remain for the U.S. economy and equity markets.

Cryptocurrencies:

The argument that crypto is a store of value like gold or would benefit from a flight to safety during period when volatility spiked, has been put to bed in recent months. After peaking at just over $125,000 in early October, Bitcoin has fallen 43% to just over $71,000. Other cryptocurrencies like Ethereum have seen sharper drops. Crypto currencies have lost just under $2 trillion in overall market value since they peaked at the start of the fourth quarter. A prudent investor has to wonder who is absorbing these losses and what, if any, impacts this could have on the equity market.

Housing:

Housing has been moribund since the impacts from the Federal Reserve launching its most aggressive monetary policy since Paul Volcker in March 2022 started to have impacts. Average mortgage rates quickly doubled from their levels in late 2021. Existing home sales from 2023-2025 came in at their lowest levels since 1995, when the U.S. population was roughly one fifth lower.

It was hoped that 2026 would finally bring a long hoped for rebound. That has not been the case. In addition to the challenges in moving existing homes, home builders are being forced to increasingly use incentives like mortgage rate buydowns to move burgeoning inventories of new homes. With incentives, the largest home builder in North America, Lennar (LEN) is seeing its lowest average selling price since 2017. Obviously, new home builders offering huge incentives, is a negative for existing home buyers being able to get close to their asking prices.

This is also taking a toll on home builders' profits and margins. KB Home (KBH) reported its latest quarterly results late in March. Homebuilding net income plunged nearly 75% year-over-year, and revenues dropped 23% from the same period a year ago.

Things are not going to get meaningfully better in the months ahead. Home sellers outnumber buyers by the largest percentage since records started to be kept in 2013 by Redfin. New home sales in January fell over 14% year-over-year. And that was before the war in the Middle East boosted mortgage rates significantly. This was just after 30-year mortgage rates had fallen to under six percent for the first time since 2022 in the back half of February.
