Japanese Bond Market:

The yield on the 10-Year Japanese sovereign debt just hit its highest level since 1997. The yield on the 30-Year bond hit is highest level ever since this instrument first started to be issued in 1999.

That is problematic on multiple fronts. The first is this could be a prelude to a debt death spiral for one of the largest economies on the globe, and one extremely impacted by the events of the Middle East. That is because Japan imports nearly all of its oil. The country gets 95% of its oil imports from the Middle East, 70% through the Strait of Hormuz.

In addition, Japan has the second oldest median age of any country and its debt to GDP ratio is roughly 230%. The yen as it is has lost just over 30% value against the dollar since 2021. Finally, the yen-carry trade has fueled global leverage for decades. As Japan has had to hike rates, the unwinding of this trade could have uncertain impacts on global equity markets.

Private Credit:

If it wasn't for the events in the Persian Gulf, financial headlines would be dominated right now by the accelerating problems in the private credit markets. Numerous large private credit funds 'gated' redemptions in March leading to some panic in this part of the credit ecosystem. These included funds run by Apollo Global Management (APO), Morgan Stanley (MS), Cliffwater, Blue Owl Capital (OWL) and KKR & Co. (KKR).

Jamie Dimon of JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) listed private credit as one of many risks for the economy and markets in 2026 with his annual shareholder letter that came out this week. The $4.9 billion Barings Private Credit Corp became the latest private credit fund to gate redemptions this week, allowing redemptions of five percent of the fund for the quarter. Far less than the 11.3% requested.

Last month, UBS projected in a worse-case scenario, default rates could hit 15% in private credit funds. This would be far higher than corporate default rates during the Great Financial Crisis it should be noted. Morgan Stanley projected a worse case default rate of 8% for private credit.

In addition, a huge chunk of private credit flows into private equity firms to acquire or recapitalize businesses, so there could be significant spillover impacts from the increasing issues within private credit. In addition, private credit makes up a decent part (in the high teens) of the market for collateralized loan obligations or CLOs.

Commercial Real Estate:

Finally, we have commercial real estate or CRE. After 15 years of the Federal Reserve maintaining near ZIRP policies and boosting M2 money supply by 40% in two years during Covid, the Fed Funds rate moved up sharply starting four years ago.

And unfortunately, a lot of bad investment allocation decisions were made when rates were near zero. And mortgages against CRE are not like the mortgage on your house. There are no 30-Year mortgages on offices or apartment complexes. The debt typically needs to be refinanced every 5-7 years. That means loans taken out between 2020-2022 are coming due and have to be refinanced at significantly higher rates.

And this is particularly impactful to the office sector, where vacancy rates have moved up sharply since the pandemic with office values collapsing in many major cities thanks largely to the explosion of the virtual workforce. The delinquency rates on Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities or CMBS on office properties have gone from under two percent in early 2022 to nearly 12%. This is higher than the peak during the Great Financial Crisis.

The CMBS delinquency rates against multi-family properties have moved above 7%, also from under 2% in 2022. The sector is being hit by record vacancies after a huge surge of apartment completions from 2023-2025, fueled by easy money from the Fed. The reverse in migration flows in 2025 is also having impacts. The results are that rents are starting to fall nationally. They fell 1.7% on a year-over-year basis in March. And this doesn't capture the increasing use of incentives (EX, two months free on a new 14-month lease) to move higher inventories.

Together office and multi-family make up approximately $3.5 trillion of the just under $5 trillion of CRE debt outstanding. Just under $900 billion of which needs to be refinanced in 2026.

Like most investors I am hoping for a complete resolution to the crisis in the Middle East. As I noted recently, a failure to open the Strait of Hormuz by the end of this month would have had major ramifications on the global economy and equity markets. However, even a peace agreement is fully implemented in April or soon thereafter, that doesn't mean investors are out of the woods. Stocks and the economy will continue to face substantial issues on numerous fronts, and many markets are already getting pummeled.

data.title: Omega Flex: Still No Catalyst In Sight
data.description:
Introduction

Omega Flex (OFLX) is a major manufacturer of flexible metal hose with marine and automotive fuel as its main applications. The company has a presence in the United States and in Europe, but unfortunately its margins have come under pressure, and this has had a negative impact on its earnings, free cash flow, and dividend coverage ratio. While the balance sheet is cash-rich (the company has about $5/share in net cash on the balance sheet), it’s not very helpful when that cash isn’t deployed properly.

While the Cash Flow Remains Strong, I Still Don’t See a Catalyst

I didn’t have high hopes for Omega Flex in 2025, and my main focus was to see how the company would deal with a stagnating market and pressure on its margins.

In 2025, Omega Flex reported a total revenue of $98.3 million, which is a 3% decrease compared to the previous financial year. Unfortunately, the total amount spent on COGS remained relatively stable (a decrease of less than 0.5%), which caused the gross profit to decline by approximately 5% to $59 million.

Unfortunately, pretty much all other operating expenses increased as well. While the increase in the selling expenses was moderate, with just 1%, G&A and engineering expenses saw a more noticeable increase, which ultimately resulted in a 20% decrease in the reported operating profit, which drifted down to just under $17 million. And to make things worse, as interest rates on the financial markets decreased throughout 2025, Omega Flex also recorded a lower interest income on its cash pile (which I will discuss in a moment).

With a net profit of $14.6 million, of which $14.8 million was attributable to the shareholders of Omega Flex (there was a $0.2 million loss attributable to non-controlling interests), the EPS of $1.47 was disappointing, as it represents a decrease of about a sixth compared to the $1.78 EPS in FY 2024.

While the generous cash dividends ($0.34 per share per quarter) were still fully covered, the payout ratio is increasing well into the 90% range, which means the dividend payments may come under pressure as well.

I do mainly focus on the free cash flow performance of a company, and that’s also what I did in my previous articles on Omega Flex. As the cash flow statement below shows, the company reported a total operating cash flow of $17.2 million, but this includes a $0.5 million contribution from changes in the working capital position, indicating the underlying operating cash flow was approximately $16.7 million.

The total capex was $1.8 million (I like companies with a low capital expenditure requirement), resulting in a net free cash flow of around $15 million, which is pretty similar to the EPS result.

One of the key elements to keep in consideration when looking at Omega Flex is the extremely strong balance sheet. The company has a positive working capital position of almost $70 million and has $53 million in cash. As there are no financial debts on the balance sheet, the $53 million in cash and cash equivalents also represents the net cash position, for more than $5/share in net cash.

While that’s a comfortable position, that cash could perhaps better be used to fund a diversification-focused acquisition, as the interest income on the cash pile is pretty minimal. Additionally, pursuing some sort of M&A to fuel growth could rekindle the interest in the company’s common shares as well.

Investment Thesis

Omega Flex remains free cash flow positive and has a very strong balance sheet with a net cash position. While that’s great to see, it’s still not good enough to warrant a market cap of over $300 million. This indeed means Omega Flex still isn’t a "Buy," and I think a "Hold" rating is more than appropriate at this time.

I will continue to keep an eye on the company and the stock, and I think using the cash pile to diversify its operations could provide a catalyst. But unfortunately that just appears to be wishful thinking on my part, as Omega Flex has not indicated that’s in the works right now.
